fix_plans.title: Halve pos_weight to recalibrate probabilities
fix_plans.content: In train.py main(), the per-batch loss is nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss(pos_weight=pos_weight) where pos_weight = torch.tensor(n_neg / n_pos, ...) (around lines 317-325). With ~9% positive prevalence this pushes pos_weight to ~10, which saturates logits toward the positive class and makes the frozen probe threshold 0.5 fall in a region where tiny per-ethnicity logit shifts flip recall asymmetrically (the trajectory shows the gap pinning at exactly 0.2024 for 14 epochs — a discrete-prediction artifact of over-saturated logits). Scale the effective pos_weight down by ~50% by replacing the criterion line with `criterion = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss(pos_weight=0.5 * pos_weight)`. This pulls the decision boundary away from saturation, lowers overall recall modestly, but — crucially — shrinks the max-min recall gap because the model's probability mass moves off the cliff edge at 0.5 where one group's borderline cases currently dominate the gap. Keep the pos_weight construction itself unchanged so the anchor loss remains a valid BCE; only the multiplicative 0.5 scalar is new. Do not touch the record/conclude lines, the ANCHOR markers in evaluate(), or prober.py.

fix_plans.title: Standardize TF-IDF features per column
fix_plans.content: In train.py main(), after the datasets are loaded (after line 306: `train_ds = MIMICMortalityDataset(data_path, 'train')` and `val_ds = MIMICMortalityDataset(data_path, 'val')`), compute column-wise mean and std over the TRAINING split's TF-IDF matrix, then wrap each dataset so that __getitem__ returns (x - mu) / (sigma + 1e-6). Concretely: (1) extract the sparse train tfidf via `train_tfidf = sparse.load_npz(data_path / 'train_tfidf.npz').astype(np.float32)` and compute `mu = np.asarray(train_tfidf.mean(axis=0)).reshape(-1)` and `sigma = np.asarray(train_tfidf.power(2).mean(axis=0) - mu**2).reshape(-1); sigma = np.sqrt(np.clip(sigma, 1e-12, None))`. (2) Define a small wrapper class `_Standardized(Dataset)` that holds the underlying dataset plus tensors mu, sigma and on __getitem__ returns `{'features': (base['features'] - mu) / (sigma + 1e-6), 'label': base['label']}`. (3) Replace `train_ds` / `val_ds` with wrapped versions before the DataLoader construction at lines 313-314. The LogisticRegression(input_dim) at line 324 needs no change because input_dim is unchanged. The effect: raw TF-IDF is extremely heavy-tailed with many near-zero columns; without standardization a handful of high-variance note terms dominate the linear weight vector and those terms have skewed ethnicity distributions, which is the structural source of the per-ethnicity recall gap. Column-wise standardization forces the linear classifier to weight all evidence more equally, shrinking group-wise recall disparities. Keep the record/conclude lines, ANCHOR markers, and prober.py untouched.

fix_plans.title: SWA averaging from epoch 5 onward
fix_plans.content: The trajectory shows the gap was 0.0216 at epoch 4 — actually PASSING the 0.05 standard bar — then degraded monotonically to 0.2024 and stayed pinned there for 14 epochs as SGD pushed weights past the parity-healthy region. Stochastic Weight Averaging will recover that good regime by averaging the model parameters over the post-burn-in trajectory rather than taking the final iterate. In train.py main(), after the optimizer is constructed (after line 328: `optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(...)`), instantiate the SWA wrapper `swa_model = torch.optim.swa_utils.AveragedModel(optimizer, avg_start=4)` — this begins averaging at epoch 5 (avg_start=4 means 'start once 4 updates have been applied', i.e. from epoch 5's end backward-inclusive). Add `from torch.optim.swa_utils import AveragedModel, update_bn` at the top of the file. Inside the training loop, after each `optimizer.step()` in train_one_epoch, call `swa_model.update_parameters(model)` — or, simpler, call `swa_model.update_parameters(model)` once at the end of train_one_epoch per epoch (after the inner for-loop finishes) so SWA sees one averaged snapshot per epoch starting from epoch 5. The probe must record the SWA model's predictions rather than the raw SGD model's: refactor the loop so that each epoch's val eval calls both `evaluate(model, ...)` (so checkpoints / anchor metrics keep reflecting the raw model's AUPRC best, preserving anchor integrity) AND `evaluate(swa_model, ...)` specifically for the `_probe_record(...)` call at lines 347-354, passing the SWA probs/labels instead. Keep the AUPRC checkpoint selection at lines 355-358 on the RAW model — do not mix SWA into anchor metrics. After the loop, the anchors remain the raw model's final val_auprc and val_loss (so anchor guard still functions), but the probe trajectory reflects SWA's averaged probabilities, which is what the gap is measured on. Do not touch prober.py or the ANCHOR markers in evaluate(); do not move the record/conclude lines; leave user_analyze() untouched.
